Let’s look at recent major hacks:

▫️ Bybit ($1.5B lost)

Got an emergency package thanks to size, reputation and $300M+ annual revenue

→ Continued operating almost immediately

▫️ Cetus ($220M lost)

$162M was frozen and recovered. Sui Foundation stepped in with a ~$30–50M secured loan

→ Cetus was back after a 17-day pause

▫️ Drift ($200M+ lost)

Drift lost 50% of its TVL. This is the second incident (after a ~$25M hack in 2022)

With an estimated revenue of ~$7M/year it's unlikely to get fundings and flash loans...

Also unlikely so far that Solana Foundation would step in at this scale

Drift can continue but rebuilding trust will be a huge challenge

DO ✅ DON'T ❌(DeFi edition)

> DO: check if a protocol has been audited before depositing anything ✅
> DON'T: ape into unaudited forks because the APY looks good ❌

> DO: check if a protocol is managed by a multisig with multiple signers (4/7 f.e) ✅
> DON'T: deposit into protocols controlled by a single wallet or unknown signer ❌

> DO: understand how the protocol sustains its yield before you deposit ✅
> DON'T: mistake inflationary token rewards for real yield ❌

> DO: check if the protocol has a timelock on admin keys ✅
> DON'T: deposit into protocols where devs can upgrade contracts instantly ❌

> DO: use TVL trends as a health check. growing = confidence. falling = caution ✅
> DON'T: deposit into a protocol just because TVL is high right now ❌

> DO: favor protocols with open source, verifiable code ✅
> DON'T: trust "trust us" projects with closed or unverified contracts❌

> DO: research who is building and for how long. public teams are accountable teams ✅
> DON'T: give serious money to fully anonymous teams with no track record expecting a magic airdrop ❌

the exploit isn't the risk. not doing your research is.

➡️ bookmark this post, apply the DO's before your next DeFi interaction. 💾

This week is a reminder that security hygiene isn't optional.

We wrote up everything you need to know about protecting your RPC tokens and endpoints: backend vs frontend auth, how tokens get leaked, and how to make sure yours don't.

The short version:
- You have two ways to authenticate your RPC endpoint: secret tokens (backend) and allowed origins (frontend)
- If your token gets leaked, anyone can route traffic through your endpoint (leading to unexpected charges)
- Treat your secret token like a private key: never log it, commit it (even to private repos), or put it in frontend code

Full guide👇 https://t.co/z4WGwmeP3T

🚨 @DriftProtocol was hit by a major exploit on April 1, with ~$270–285M drained from vaults within ~1 hour via suspicious transactions.

Initial findings suggest the attacker leveraged pre-signed durable nonce transactions combined with possible social engineering (rather than a typical smart contract bug), moving funds across chains to Ethereum to obfuscate flows.

Drift has paused deposits/withdrawals, confirmed an active attack, and is working with security partners and exchanges to contain damage while investigations continue — users are advised to revoke approvals and stay alert as the situation develops.

UPDATE: According to @zachxbt, $230M+ USDC was bridged from Solana to Ethereum across 100+ transactions, while @Circle had a 6-hour window to freeze funds following the $280M+ Drift exploit but remained silent. https://t.co/8ClbztLMh1

🚨JUST IN: Initial findings on the @DriftProtocol exploit:

➡️A highly coordinated attacker gained control of Drift’s Security Council admin using pre-signed transactions via durable nonce accounts, indicating weeks of preparation and staged execution.

➡️The attack was not due to any smart contract bug or exploit, and there is no evidence of compromised seed phrases.

➡️Instead, the attacker appears to have obtained 2/5 multisig approvals in advance, likely through sophisticated social engineering or misrepresented transaction approvals.

➡️As early as March 23, multiple durable nonce accounts were set up across both multisig members and attacker-controlled wallets, allowing transactions to be signed earlier and executed later.

➡️Even after a Security Council multisig migration on March 27, the attacker regained effective access to required signers, showing persistent compromise.

➡️On April 1, shortly after a legitimate insurance fund test transaction, the attacker executed two pre-signed nonce transactions just minutes apart, enabling a rapid and near-instant admin takeover.

➡️With full admin control, the attacker introduced a malicious asset, removed withdrawal limits, and exploited protocol permissions to drain funds.

➡️Approximately $280M was withdrawn, impacting all funds in borrow/lend, vault deposits, and trading balances on the platform.

➡️Unaffected funds include DSOL not deposited into Drift and Insurance Fund assets, which are being moved for safeguarding.

➡️All protocol functions have now been frozen, and the compromised multisig wallet has been removed to prevent further damage.

➡️Drift is actively coordinating with security firms, bridges, exchanges, and law enforcement to trace and potentially recover stolen assets, with a full postmortem expected soon.

🚨 Drift Hack TLDR (plain english)

A hacker group likely linked to North Korea planned the whole hack for about 20 days

Here's what they did, step by step:

1. They made up a worthless token called CVT.  
Then they tricked an oracle into thinking this fake coin was super valuable — worth over $100 million. (In reality, it was almost worthless).

2. They used a sneaky computer virus (hidden in a software update from a company called Axios) to infect someone's computer. 
This gave them access to a special "security key" belonging to someone on Drift's (security) team.

3. With that stolen key, they pretended to put up their fake $100M+ CVT as collateral.  
Then they borrowed "real" money (over $200 million worth of different coins) from Drift's system.

4. They quickly moved the stolen money through several "bridges" and services (like Circle, Wormhole, Mayan, and etc.) to hide it and cash it out.

5. Luckily, something in the system (called a "noop program") kicked in and blocked the full theft. Not all the TVL is gone.

MetaTimer: Using Large Language Models for Precise, Prompt-Aware Inference Latency Prediction

The rapid proliferation of large language models (LLMs) in production systems has exposed a fundamental limitation: inference latency varies dramatically across prompts due to differences in semantic complexity, required reasoning depth, output length, and generation dynamics. Conventional prediction methods—ranging from token-count heuristics and hardware Roofline models to traditional machine-learning regressors—fail to generalize because they cannot capture these prompt-specific nuances. Accurate a priori estimation of processing time is essential for resource scheduling, dynamic batching, cost forecasting, service-level guarantees, and user-experience enhancements.

We introduce MetaTimer, the first framework to repurpose a lightweight LLM itself as a high-precision meta-predictor capable of forecasting the exact wall-clock inference duration required by any target LLM for an arbitrary input prompt. A compact 8B-parameter model is fine-tuned on a massive corpus of millions of prompt–execution pairs collected across heterogeneous model families (GPT-4-class, Llama 3.1, Claude, Mistral), quantization levels, decoding strategies, and hardware accelerators. The predictor employs chain-of-thought reasoning to decompose prompt semantics, estimate output token distributions and reasoning trajectories, and integrate model- and hardware-specific performance profiles, yielding fine-grained predictions for Time-to-First-Token (TTFT), Time-Per-Output-Token (TPOT), and total latency.

Extensive evaluations on held-out benchmarks spanning reasoning, creative writing, coding, and long-context tasks demonstrate state-of-the-art accuracy: a mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of 6.3% for end-to-end latency—representing a >40% reduction in mean squared error relative to the strongest Roofline–ML baselines—and strong zero-shot generalization to unseen models and platforms. When integrated into production serving stacks (vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, Triton), MetaTimer delivers up to 31% gains in resource utilization and tail-latency reduction.

These results establish that LLMs possess emergent capabilities for computational self-modeling, opening a new paradigm for self-aware, adaptive, and energy-efficient generative AI infrastructure. We publicly release the predictor model, dataset, and serving plugins to accelerate research in meta-performance modeling for frontier AI systems.

80085-sol, three times. $35, $42, $149. same token, same strat, just kept farming it.

narrative was great last night and the token survived, so i kept going. volume was sitting at 200k-350k per 5 minutes, TVL was low, pool was on 5% base fee. that combination is exactly what you want for spot tight. 

pool link in the comments if you want to farm this too.

the strat is spot -10%, single side sol. the exit rule is strict: when price touches about half of my position and starts going out of range.

i withdraw and reposition if it's still pumping. no waiting, no hoping it comes back in range. out, reassess, redeploy.

that's why the third position held for 6 minutes and made $149. the first two were quick cycles, the third one caught a bigger move and i let it run until the same exit rule triggered.

but this strat needs real self-control. the moment you start thinking "let me hold a bit more" or "it'll come back in range" you've already broken the logic.

the whole edge is in executing the exit exactly when the condition hits, not when it feels right.

if you don't trust yourself to exit on the rule, this strat won't work for you. it's that simple.

while QCs are still very far out and while I think Bitcoin *will* be ready by then, it is an insane failure of basic brain function to equate bitcoin to a bank when it suits you but then say it's much superior to a bank when it doesn't 

i) bitcoin is secured by cryptography, banks are secured by trust. this is a cryptographic attack. you can not call the bitcoin support agent to freeze your shit, you are explicitly and unilaterally trusting cryptography 

ii) banks do not publicly publish pubkeys to be decrypted, bitcoin does

iii) bank txns are controlled and reversible, bitcoin txns are not

iv) banks can push an update in seconds, bitcoin cannot

v) a bank has central monitoring systems, bitcoin does not (and for this attack, physically can not), an attack wouldn't even be attributable in a decentralized system and would be indistinguishable from random ppl misplacing their seed phrases

you can keep the attack going without ever being detected 

vi) a bank can take action on your account without you needing to do anything. this is much, much messier for a blockchain

a single ledger is infinitely easier to control than thousands of ledgers, that's kind of the entire point of this industry 

again, the qc risk is overblown forsure but assuming its absense is perhaps the biggest risk of all

a medium of exchange is not the defining characteristic of money

money is not what you transact in but what you think in

random thought that I just got after looking up money in the dictionary

the way you learn about money is through the barter problem (which is incorrect btw)

that is, I have potatoes and you have corn, and if I want corn and you want potatoes, then we trade

but if I don't want corn and you don't want potatoes, then we can't trade. therefore, we designate a medium of exchange and then use that for all other assets as well

the coincidence of double wants problem solved!

but now imagine you have tokenized all assets onchain and you have a colossal AMM that has all pairs

i.e corn/wheat, wheat/potatoes, potatoes/oil, oil/steel

ok now you have also solved the coincidence of double wants, you can trade any asset for any other asset

but there's still a problem, you can't make denominate rates consistently and thus you can't coordinate the economy

also, any asset that is not seasonal will not lose value with time and thus will get a monetary premium (hence gold) which will skew the price signal

therefore, the main functions must be i) unit of account, ii) store of value with iii) medium of exchange being a secondary function, in that order

or more succinctly, money is not the thing you transact in but the thing you think in

anime-sol, 43.78%. this is the tight bid-ask flip wide strat and it's one of my favorite plays on a strong runner.

the setup has two phases.

phase 1: tight bid-ask. you need a strong runner with huge volume and solid narrative that's already dumped around 40% from ath. 

pool link : https://t.co/Yf52Rgoclv

that's your entry window. deploy bid-ask with a tight range, -7% to -15% depending on how confident you are in the token. the goal here is simple — let your SOL convert to tokens as price sweeps through your bins on the way down.

once your SOL is fully converted to tokens, you close the position.

phase 2: flip to token side wide. open a new position using token side only with a 100-300% range to the upside. this wide range lets you DCA in and out as the token recovers. 

you're now sitting with tokens, collecting fees as the price moves back up through your range.

why not just use a normal wide range from the start? because on a strong runner with good volume the TVL gets absolutely crowded. 

tight range on the way down gets you in efficiently before the pool thickens. flipping to wide on token side gives you the recovery play without competing with everyone else for the same bins.

the whole thing only works if the narrative is genuinely strong. if the token is just a one-day wonder this falls apart at phase 2. pick the right token and the recovery almost always comes.
